text: ANDIA, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Malwah, 45 m. NNE of Bhopal.

ANDIAN, a town of Independent Tartary, in Great Bukharia, 15 m. N of Nash-Gherd. ANDIJAUN, ANDEGAN, or FERGANAH, a town and fortress of Independent Tartary, in the prov. of Kokan, and district of Ferganah, on the l. bank of the Sihon, 40 m. ESE of Kokan, in N lat. 41° 15'.

ANDILLA, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Valencia, situated in a mountainous district 41 m. NW of Valencia. It owes its existence to James I., king of Arragon, by whom it was built. It has a church which contains some good paintings by Castenada and Ribalta. Pop. 500.
ANDILLY, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Seine et Oise, and arrond. of Pontoise, 13 m. NW of Montmorency. It is celebrated as the birth-place of Arnaud. Pop. 364.
ANDILLY-LE-MARAIS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Charente-Inférieure, arrond. of La Rochelle. Pop. 1,337.
ANDIOL (SAINT), a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Bouches-du-Rhone, and arrond. d'Arles; 4 m. SSE of Avignon. Pop. 1,080.
ANDISLEBEN, or Hassleben, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of Saxony, and gov. of Erfurth, on the r. bank of the Gera, 8 m. N of Erfurth.

ANDJE-KARASU, or Indje-Karasu, a river of Greece, in Macedonia, formed by the confluence of the united streams of the Venetico and the Phili with the Nazilitza. It runs in a NE direction to Egribudpak; and, after receiving the Vendjia, bends towards the gulf of Salonica, which it enters by two principal estuaries, in about N lat. 40° 31', and E long. 22° 37′.

ANDKHU, a town of Independent Tartary, in the prov. of Balkh, on the r. bank of a tributary of the Oxus, in N lat. 36° 54'. A. is the capital of a small independent khanat, formerly belonging to Cabul; and contains about 400 houses. The pop., consisting principally of Arabs, is estimated at 1,136. The enterprising Moorcroft died here.

ANDLAU, a small river of France, in the dep. of Bas-Rhin. It takes its rise in the Vosges, to the N of the village of Steige, passes Andlau and St. Pierre, and falls into the Ill, about 7 m. to the S of Strasburg, after a NE course of 24 m. It is navigable from the town of the same name to the Ill. Its principal traffic is in transporting timber from the forests of the Vosges.

ANDLAU, a small but ancient town of France, in the dep. of Bas-Rhin, and arrond. of Schelestadt, near the source of the above river, 18 m. SSW of Strasburg. Cotton-spinning is the principal industrial occupation of the inhabitants of A. This town was anciently held by the barons of Andlau as a fief under the abbess of the convent, who bore the title of Princess of the Roman empire, had a seat among the Rhenish prelates, and held her authority direct from the pope. The ancient baronial castle still exists. Pop. 2,179.

ANDO, a small lake of Russia, in the NE confines of the prov. of Olonetz, and about 23 m. E of Lake Vigo.—Also a town on the W side of the lake. ANDO, ANDOEN, or HINDOE, the most northerly of the Lofoden islands, off the NE coast of Norway. It is 35 m. in length from N to S; and 10 m. in breadth. Central lat. 69° 20' N., and long. 15° 30' E. It rises 3,200 ft. above the snow-line.

ANDOLSHEIM, a canton, commune, and village of France, in the dep. of Haut-Rhin, and arrond. of Colmar. The cant. comprises 18 com., and in 1831 contained a pop. of 13,137.—The v. lies about 3 m. to the E of Colmar. It has a consistorial Protestant church. Pop. 1,046.

ANDOMA, a navigable river of Russia, in the gov. of Olonetz, and district of Vitegra. It takes its rise in a small lake near the northern confines of the district; and, skirting a mountain-ridge, falls, after a course of 72 m., into the SE side of Lake Onego, about 33 m. N of Vitegra. Its principal affluents are, on the right, the Tukhta, Nozdragna, and Somina; and on the left the Lundojma and Berezovka.—Also a large village on the l. bank of the Andoma, 24 m. NNE of Vitegra. It has a pottery manufactory.

ANDONEG, a river of Upper Guinea, in the district of Warre, which falls into the bight of Biafra, at the village of the same name, about 18 m. to the E of the Bonny. It is supposed to be one of the estuaries of the Quorra.

ANDORA, a town of Italy, in the Sardinian territory of Genoa, the cap. of the district of Albenga. It is situated on the crest of a mountain near the W shore of the gulf of Genoa, and at the opening of a rich valley watered by a river of the same name, 5 m. NE of Oneglia. The principal productions of the environs are wines and oil.
ANDORGOE, an island of Norway, separated by a narrow strait from the coast of Norland. It is about 12 m. long and 6 broad.

ANDORNO-CACCIORNA, a commune and town of Piedmont, the cap. of the district of Biella, 25 m. NW of Vercelli. It is situated on the Cervo, in a valley containing 10 villages, with a pop. of 12,000, employed in working the copper and lead mines of the adjacent mountains. A. has a communal college. This valley is celebrated as the birth-place of the painter Cagliari.
ANDOROSSA. See Andrussa.

ANDORRA, or ANDORRE, a small independent state composed of three of the wildest and most picturesque vallies on the southern side of the Central Pyrenees, forming a basin with its embouchure stretching towards the Spanish Urgel, and enclosed by mountains whose peaks are amongst the highest and most inaccessible of this range. It is bounded on the N by Ariège; on the E by the district of Carol; on the S by that of Urgel; and on the W by the valley of Paillas. Its extent from N to S is about 36 m.; its average breadth 30 m. [Murray.] It contains six communes or parishes: viz., Andorre, AND Canillo, Enchamp, La Massane, Urdino, and St. Julien; and above 30 villages or hamlets. The village of Andorra, or Andorre-la-Vielle, from which the republic takes its name, is the principal and probably the most ancient. It is situated on the Balira; and contains a pop. of about 2,000—or, according to Ford, of only 1,000. A. is an extremely mountainous district; and its unequal surface is to a great extent bristled with rocks. It is watered by several rivers rising within its confines, of which the principal is the Balira or Embalire, which, after having received all the minor streams, falls into the Segre, a branch of the Ebro. The Val d'Urdino is the richest and most fertile of the lesser vallies. The mountains are generally covered with forests of pine, and afford abundant pasturage for the numerous herds of cattle which are reared by the inhabitants. Bears, wolves, wild boars, goats, and various species of game, abound in the mountains. The chief industrial pursuit of the inhabitants is the smelting of iron. At Ransol, in the commune of Canillo, is an iron-mine, and there are forges in the villages of Encamp, Caldes, and Urdino, and in the hamlet of Serrat. [Rev. Encyc.] Manufactures are limited to those of the most indispensable articles. Commerce is entirely free; but Andorra has little to offer in exchange with other countries except its iron, the whole of which it exports to Spain; high duties prohibiting its entrance into France. Timber is floated down the Balira and Segre to Tortosa. The Andorrians, who are in general robust and well-proportioned, are extremely primitive in their habits, and simple and severe in their manners. An industrious and contented race, cherishing a high spirit of independence, they know few wants which the meagre resources of their little country do not supply. Their number does not exceed 14,000. Ford estimates it at only 8,000. Their wealth generally consists in a few sheep or cattle, and perhaps a few shares in the iron-forges. A few only are proprietors of any extent of land beyond the little garden which surrounds their cottage. The costume of the men, composed of coarse brown cloth made from the wool of their own sheep, resembles that of the peasants of Bigorre, with the flowing red cap of the Catalans. The dress of the women is exactly that of Catalonia. Crime is rare; but its punishment is condign and generally efficient. Murder is a crime extremely rare in this little state; and, when sentence of death is pronounced, it cannot be executed until it is confirmed by a general assembly of representatives of the villages convoked at A. The mode of execution is consistent with the nature of the people. At a short distance from the road into Catalonia is a tremendous precipice, the bottom of which no eye of man can discover: the criminal, with his eyes bound, is led to the edge, and, in the presence of all who wish to attend, is thrown over by the executioner. All the males are obliged to serve in the defence of their country; and the head of each family must be provided with a certain supply of firearms and ammunition. Each commune has a captain and subordinate officers. Each family recognises a chief, who succeeds, by unalienable right of primogeniture, to all its property, and who can only contract marriage in families of equal consideration. If the representative of a family be a daughter, her name is adopted by her husband,-unless he be an eldest son, in which case their names are conjoined, -and he is domiciliated in her family. Thus the principal Andorrian families have existed, with little change of fortune, during the lapse of many centuries,—one it is said as long as 800 years. An eldest son is always entitled to a certain superior status in his father's house; but until he is married, he is not at liberty to leave it, nor to undertake any charge of public affairs. Situated on the southern side of the Pyrenees, and beyond the natural frontier of France, A. ought, from its natural position, to belong to Spain. It is however considered as a neutral and independent province; although to a certain extent connected with both countries. It is under the spiritual jurisdiction of the bishop of Urgel, to whom it pays an annual sum of 480 francs; while it renders to France an annual tribute of double that amount in consideration of certain privileges which it enjoys under her protection. The government is composed of a council of 24 members, of whom each commune elects 4, who are chosen for life. The council elect a syndic or procurator-general, in whom the executive power is permanently vested, and who, except during the sittings of the council-which take place five times in the year-has the entire government of the community. The judicial administration is conducted by two viguiers, [vicars or lieutenants,] one of whom is appointed by the bishop of Urgel, and must be an Andorrian; the other, who is generally the justice-ofpeace for the canton of Ax, is nominated by France. The office of these functionaries is gratuitous. Criminal cases are tried, without much regard to legal formalities, by the viguiers assisted by two members of the supreme council, and the civil judge of appeal, as councillors. The civil judges are elected by the viguiers, and hold office for three years. When Napoleon crossed the Pyrenees, on his way into Spain, he stopped at A., the capital, and promised to confer on the republic a written code of laws; but this promise concurrent political events prevented him from performing. The inhabitants at length framed a code for themselves, and it was recently promulgated at the chief town. It is of the greatest simplicity, comprising all its enactments, civil and criminal, in 100 articles only.—The Andorrians are strict adherents of the church of Rome. The clergy are generally natives of the republic; and they, and the more wealthy of the lay members of the community, receive their education at Toulouse or Barcelona. To each cure is attached a school in which the poor receive gratuitous instruction, but the peasantry are generally very ignorant. The vernacular language is the Catalan.—The republic of A. presents the remarkable phenomenon of a little country, locked between two powerful kingdoms, having preserved, with its independence, the same institutions for a long series of ages in the midst of the political convulsions by which it has so often been surrounded. The Andorri, or Andorrisæ, are spoken of by Pliny, who locates them in the environs of Cadiz. About the year 790, in return for services rendered to Charlemagne in his march through the mountainous defiles of Catalonia, the Andorrensis pagus was erected by that prince into an independent state. Their present system of administration they received from Louis le Debonnaire, who, after having driven the Moors beyond the Ebro, ceded to the bishop of Urgel—who thus became entitled to the tithes of the 6 parishes—a portion of those rights over A. which his father had reserved to himself and his successors. The name Andorra, according to Ford, is derived from the Arabic Aldarra, which signifies 'a place thick with trees.'— Revue Encyclopedique, 1823.—Malte Brun.—Balbi.— Roussillon, Notice sur l'Andorre, 1832. - Murray's Summer in the Pyrenees, 2 vols. 8vo. These volumes contain a very interesting account of this little republic, of which our article is mainly an abridgment.
ANDOSILLA, a town of Spain, in Navarre, 6 m. from Calaharra, near the Ega river.

ANDOVER, a borough, market-town, and parish, in the hundred of Andover, Southamptonshire. It | AND stands near the l. bank of the Anton, on the border of the Wiltshire downs, and near the edge of an extensive tract of woodland, 63 m. WSW from London; 25 m. N by W from Southampton; in N lat. 51° 12½, W long. 1° 28'. It is a neat and well-built place, consisting of three principal streets. There was formerly an extensive manufacture of shalloon here, which has been superseded by that of silk. A considerable trade in corn, malt, and timber, is conducted chiefly by means of a canal through Stockbridge to Southampton water. A. is considerably benefited by the vicinity of Weyhill, about 3 m. W of the town, where a great annual fair of 14 days is held at Michaelmas. The Andover-road station on the Southwestern railway is 56 m. from London, and 22 m. from Southampton. A. unites with the tything of Foxcote and the parish of Knight's-Enham in returning two members to parliament. The pop. of the parl. burgh in 1841 was 4,997; of the parish, which includes 7 hamlets, and comprises an area of 7,670 acres, 4,941. Andover is a corruption of the Saxon Andeafaran, signifying 'the passage of the Ande,' or Anton. In the church of this place, Anlaf, king of Norway, in 994, received the sacrament of confirmation, under the sponsorship of King Ethelred. The Roman road from Winchester to Cirencester passed near A., and is still visible in some places. There are several Roman encampments in the neighbourhood, especially one on the summit of Buryhill, 2 m. to the SSW. The earl of Suffolk takes the title of viscount from Andover.-The hundred of A., comprising 16 parishes, with an area of 33,820 acres, had a pop. in 1841 of 5,161. The division of A., with an area of 80,830 acres, had a pop. of 13,245.

ANDOVER, a town of Massachusetts, U. S., in the co. of Essex, 21 m. N of Boston; 16 NW of Salem. Pop. in 1820, 3,889; in 1830, 4,540; in 1840, 5,207. It is pleasantly situated on the SE side of the Merrimack, and is also watered by the Shawsheen. It has some inconsiderable manufactories; but is chiefly celebrated for its literary institutions, and particularly for its theological seminary, founded in 1807, which is under a president and 4 professors; is attended by about 140 students; and has a library of 20,000 vols. Philips' academy, founded in 1788, is well endowed, and affords education to 132 students.—There are several townships in the United States bearing this name.—In Maine, co. Oxford. Pop. 551.—In New Hampshire, co. Merrimack, 21 m. from Concord. Pop. 1,168.—In Vermont, co. Windsor, 20 m. SW of Windsor. Pop. 877.-In New York, co. Alleghany, 257 m. W by S of Albany. Pop. 848.—In New Jersey, co. Sussex, near the source of the Pequest, 10 m. S of Newton.-In Ohio, co. Ashtabula, 200 m. NE of Columbus. Pop. 881.
ANDOUILLE', a commune and village of France, dep. of Mayenne, cant. of Chailland, near the Ernée river, 6 m. N of Laval. Pop. 2,751. Iron-mines are worked here.

ANDRA, or Andrastadt (Saint), a town of Austria, in Carinthia, in the circle and 30 m. E by N of Klagenport, on the Lavant river. Pop. 700. It is the seat of the bishop of Lavant, a suffragan of Salzburg.—Also a town of Hungary, in the circle of Pesth, on the r. bank of the Danube. Pop. 3,000.

ANDRADE (Rio), a town of La Plata, prov. of Cordova, near the source of a river of the same name, 66 m. N of Cordova, in S lat. 30° 25', W long. 62° 45'. The river joins the Rio Seco, about 35 m. below the town.
ANDRAGIRI. See SUMATRA.
ANDRAIX, or ANDRACIO, a town on the W coast of the island of Majorca, 12 m. W of Palma. Pop. 3,500. Olives are extensively cultivated here.

ANDRAKI, the port of the ancient city Myra, in Asiatic Turkey, on the South coast of Caramania, at 1 the mouth of the Andraki, "a small brackish river," in N lat. 36° 13', E long. 30°. Captain Beaufort identifies this place with the Andriace of Ptolemy. The ruins of Myra are about 3 m. up the river.
ANDRAMA, a town of Asiatic Turkey, prov. of Algezira, 34 m. SSW of Jezireh.
ANDRARUM, or ANDERUM, a town of Sweden, prov. of Christianstadt, on the coast, 14 m. S of Christianstadt. Pop. in 1805, 376.

ANDRAS (SAINT), a village of Hungary, on the Banat, 12 m. NW of Temesvar. Between this v. and that of Kis Becskrek lies the field of battle on which the fate of the Hungarian struggle for independence, in 1848, was chiefly decided, by Haynau defeating Bem and Guvon.
ANDRAUSSA, or Andrusa, a town of Greece, in the Morea, 24 m. NE of Navarino.

ANDRE' (SAINT), a commune of Belgium in Liege. Pop. 521.—Also a canton, commune, and village of France, in the dep. of Basses-Alpes, arrond. of Castellane. The cant., comprising 10 com., had a pop. of 3,395 in 1831. The com., 8 m. N of Castellane, has a pop. of 785.—Also a com. in the dep. of Hautes- Alpes, arrond. of Embrun. Pop. 1,051.—Also a com. and v. in the dep. of Aveyron, arrond. of Villefranchede-Rouergne. Pop. 2,000.—Also a cant., com., and v. in the dep. of Eure, arrond. of Evreux. The cant., comprising 38 com., had a pop. in 1831 of 12,976. The com., 10 m. from Evreux, has a pop. of 1,202.— There is a hamlet of the same name in the com. of Cléry, dep. of Liaret. Pop. 840.—Also a com. in the dep. of Nievre, arrond. of Clamecy. Pop. 1,301.— Also a com. in the dep. of Nord, cant. of Lille. Pop. 538.—Also a com. in the dep. of Puy-de-Dome, cant. of Randans. Pop. 1,000.—Also a com. in the Pyrenees-Orientales, cant. of Argeles. Pop. 511. Excellent white wines are made here.
ANDRE'-D'ARCHON (SAINT), a com. and v. of France, in the dep. of Loire, arrond, and 7 m. W of Roanne. Pop. 1,747.
ANDRE'-DE-CHALANÇON (SAINT), a com. and v. in the dep. of Haute-Loire, cant. of Bas-en-Basset. Pop. 1,165.
ANDRE'-DES-COMBES (SAINT), a com. and v. in the dep. of Charente, cant. and 4 m. W by N of Cognac. Pop. 298.

ANDRE'-DE-CUBZAC (SAINT), a canton, commune, and village of France, in the dep. of Gironde. —The cant., comprising 11 com., had a pop. of 9,021 in 1831.—The com. and vil. are on the Dordogne river, 11 m. NE of Bordeaux. Pop. 2,957. Wine is extensively manufactured here.
ANDRE'-LE-DESERT (SAINT), a commune in the dep. of Saone-et-Loire, cant. of Cluny. Pop. 1,130.
ANDRE'-DES-EAUX (SAINT), a commune in the dep. of Loire-Inférieure, cant. of Guérande. Pop. 1,379.
ANDRE'-GOULDOCE (SAINT), a commune in the dep. of Vendée, cant. of Saint-Fulgent. Pop. 1,072.
ANDRE'-DE-LEDON (SAINT), a commune in the dep. of Charente-Inferieure, cant. of Gemozac. Pop. 1,380.
ANDRE'-DE-MACHACA, a town in Upper Peru, prov. of La Paz, on a branch of the Desaguadero, 53 m. SW of La Paz.
ANDRE'-DE-MAGENAULES, a commune in the dep. of Gard, cant. of Valleraugue. Pop. 1,722.
ANDRE'-DE-LA-MARCHE (SAINT), a commune in the dep. of Maine-et-Loire, cant. of Montfaucon. Pop. 1,049.
ANDRE'-D'ORNAIS (SAINT), a commune in the dep. of Vendée, cant. of Bourbon-Vendée. Pop. 713. AND dep. of Isère, cant. of Pont-de-Beauvoisin. Pop. 1,144.
ANDRE'-DE-SANGONIS (SAINT), a commune in the dep. of Herault, cant. of Arboras. Pop. 2,131.
ANDRE'-TREIZE-VOIES (SAINT), a commune in the dep. of Vendée, cant. of Rocheserviere. Pop. 1,139.

ANDRE'-DE-VALBORGNE (SAINT), a canton, commune, and town in the dep. of Gard, arrond. of Le Vigan.—The cant., which comprises 5 com., had a pop. in 1831 of 4,407.—The town is 14 m. NE of Le Vigan. Pop. 1,854.
ANDRE'-DE-VESINES (SAINT), a commune in the dep. of Aveyron, cant. of Peyreleau. Pop. 2,019.
ANDRE'A, a town of Upper Guinea, 7 or 8 m. from the mouth of the Mesurado. It is the capital of a district, but an insignificant place.

ANDREA (St.), a small island in the Adriatic, on the Dalmatian coast, W of Lissa, in N lat. 43°, E long. 15° 45'.—Also a village of Naples, 3 m. NE of Conza. Pop. 2,200.—Also a cape forming the NE extremity of the island of Cyprus, in E long. 34° 41'. It rises in a lofty peak called St. Croix, bearing NW from Larnica.

ANDREAN, or Andreanof Isles, a group of islands in the North Pacific, forming part of the Aleutian archipelago. Central lat. 52° N. They lie between the Aleutians properly so called, and the Fox group; and are fourteen in number, besides a multitude of islets mere rocks.
ANDREAS, a parish in the isle of Man, 3 m. NW of Ramsay. Pop. 2,332. Houses 435.

ANDREASBERG, a town of Hanover, prov. of Grubenhagen, in the Upper Hartz, 15 m. N of Goslar, on the side of a mountain of the same name, at an alt. of 1,872 ft. Pop. 4,300. The neighbourhood is rich in mines of silver, lead, copper and iron, which have been wrought here since the beginning of the 16th century. ANDREEWA, ANDREWA, or ENDERI, a district in the Russian Caucasus, embracing the peninsula and gulf of Agraschanskoi, between the Aksai river and the Caspian. Its capital, of the same name, at the foot of Mount Shumlu, contains a pop. of about 12,000.
ANDRES, or Anderes, a town of Asiatic Turkey, prov. of Konieh, 60 m. E of Angora.—Also a com. and vil. of France, dep. of Pas-de-Calais, cant. of Guines, 6 m. SE of Calais. Pop. 873.
ANDRES (SAN). See Brazos (LITTLE).
ANDRES DE AGUERA (SAN), a valley of Spain, in Asturias, 12 m. from Piedrafita. It contains several hamlets, with a united pop. of about 900.
ANDRES ITZAPA (SAN), a town of Guatemala, in the alcaldia of Chimaltenango. Pop. 1,500, who cultivate wheat, maize, pulse and vegetables.
ANDRES DE NUEVA ZARAGOZA (SAN), a mining-village of Guatemala, in the district of Comayagna, on a mountain to the W of Gracias a Dios. A rich gold mine was discovered here in 1544.
ANDRES XECUL (SAN), a village of Guatemala, in the district of Totonicapan. Pop. 1,200.
ANDRESY, a commune and village of France, dep. of Seine-et-Oise, cant. and 1 m. NE of Poissy. Pop. 919.
ANDRETTA, a town of Naples, prov. of Principato Ultra, at the base of Monte Gatello; 24 m. SSW of Ascoli. Pop. 4,609.
ANDREVSKAIA, a bay of Siberia, gov. of Tobolsk, on the Arctic ocean, in N lat. 76° 20', E long. 96° 14′.

ANDREW'S (SAINT), an ancient city and seaport town of Fifeshire; the seat of the oldest of the four Scottish universities. It stands on a rocky promontory, at the bottom of a small bay of the same name,

ANDRE'-LA-PALUD (SAINT), a commune in the on the S side of the firth of Tay, 39 m. NE from Edinburgh, in N lat. 56° 19', W long. 2° 50'. It was erected into a royal burgh by David I., in 1140; and has been the scene of many memorable transactions in the civil and ecclesiastical history of Scotland. About the same time it was rendered an archiepiscopal seat, and became the resort of many religious orders. The archb. of St. A. took precedence of all other peers, was perpetual moderator of all national synods, and supreme judge in almost all civil and criminal cases within his numerous regalities. The cathedral church, begun in 1159, and completed at 1318, was a splendid building, but was demolished in the Reformation. The castle-of which little now remains—was built on a rock projecting into the sea. It is famous as the scene of several events in Scottish history, particularly of the assassination of Cardinal Beaton in 1545.—The university of St. Andrews was founded by Bishop Wardlaw in 1411. It formerly consisted of three colleges; that of St. Salvator, founded in 1455; St. Leonard's, founded in 1512; and St. Mary's, completed in 1561. St. Salvator's and St. Leonard's were conjoined in 1747. The annual revenue of the university is about £3,000. The total number of students seldom exceeds 300. The library contains nearly 40,000 vols. The Madras college, founded by Dr. Bell in 1832, is a richly endowed institution. Its buildings form a handsome quadrangle.—The burgh of St. A. consists of three principal streets, and a few lanes and detached houses, interspersed with gardens. Its total area, gardens included, is 109 acres. Pop. in 1841, 3,959. Houses 923. It once had an extensive foreign trade; but now not above 10 or 12 small coasting-vessels belong to its port, which is a bonding-port. Its manufactures consist of Osnaburghs, canvass, and linen; the making of golf-balls also employs a few hands. The parliamentary burgh of St. A., which had a pop. of 4,449 in 1841, conjointly with six neighbouring burghs, whose united pop. in 1841, with that of St. A., amounted to 15,182, returns a member to parliament. The parish of St. A., which is about 10 m. in length and 3 broad, is generally fertile, and in a high state of cultivation. Its pop. in 1831 was 5,621; in 1841, 6,017.

ANDREW'S (SAINT), a frontier town of New Brunswick, on the E side of the inner bay of Passamaquoddy, 195 m. by road SE from Quebec, 67 m. W by S by road from St. John's. It is seated at the bottom of a ridge of hills at the mouth of the Schoodiac, or, as the Americans call it, the St. Croix, which river separates New Brunswick from the United States. The town is clean and regularly built, with main streets at equal distances and parallel to each other and to the shore. Pop. 4,000. The timber-trade, and traffic with the W Indian islands, is the great business of the place. An extensive contraband trade is said to exist here. A railway is about to be formed from this place to Woodstock, a distance of 80 m.; and from the latter place to the Grand falls, where it will run into the projected main trunk line from Halifax to Quebec, while on the United States side it is proposed to push forward a line from Boston and Augusta to St. A. The harbour of St. A. is capacious, deep, well-protected, and never closed in the winter-season, whilst the St. Lawrence is unnavigable from November to May; but a waggon railway between Quebec and St. A. would save a navigation of 1,200 m., and convey the merchandise of the St. Lawrence in a single day to the Atlantic. The average run of steamers from St. John's to St. A. is 7 hours. The rise in the tide here is not less than 25 ft. perpendicular.
ANDREW ROSS, an island on the W side of the gulf of Boothia, in N lat. 70° 12'.

ANDRIA, a town of Naples, prov. of Terra di | Smolensk, 20 m. SE of Krasnoia, on the Gorodnia. AND Bari, 9 m. S of Barletta, 31 m. NW of Bari. It is a bishop's see, suffragan of Trani; and has the title of duchy. Pop, 6,000.—Also a town of Russia, gov. of Circassia, prov. of Daghestan, 90 m. NW of Derbend.
ANDRICHOL (CAPE), a headland on the SW coast of Majorca, 2 m. N by W of Malgrat, the coast between forming the bay of Santa Ponza. Its summit is covered with pine trees.
ANDRIES, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Yonne, cant. of Coulange-sur-Yonne. Pop. 1,085.—Also a hamlet on the island of Bommel in Dutch Gueldres.

ANDRITZENA, a town of the Morea, 31 hours from Tragoï, 7 m. SE of Phanari; in N lat. 37° 29'. It is finely situated in an elevated hollow under Mount Ai Elia or St. Elias, at the head of a hilly but fertile tract which slopes towards the Alpheus. Its staple article of commerce is prinokokki, which is gathered from the Kermes oak in the neighbouring mountains [Leake]. When Colonel Harriot visited this place in 1831 it had only 150 houses.
ANDRIUSHKIVA, a small river of Russia, in the gov. of Archangel, rising in the country of the Samoiedes, and running S into the Petshora.

ANDRO, the ancient Andros, an island of the Cyclades, in the Grecian archipelago, about 21 m. in length, and 6 m. in breadth; divided from Tino by a narrow strait. Pop. about 18,000. The NW point, called Point Guardia, is in N lat. 37° 57', E long. 24° 42'. It is high and mountainous; and the summits of the mountains retain the snow during several months of the year; but in the plains, wine, oil, wheat, and barley, are raised, and figs, olives, oranges, lemons, and pomegranates, in such quantities as to admit of exportation. The main production is silk, of which about 3,000 lbs. are exported annually. -The cap. of the same name-sometimes also called Castro—is at the head of a bay on the E coast, in N lat. 37° 49′, E long. 24° 56′.
ANDRONA, a district in the interior of Madagascar, between the countries of the Seclaves and the Antsianaxes.

ANDROS, a large island in the Bahama group, extending 90 m. from N by W to S by E, and 35 m. in extreme breadth. Its NE point is in N lat. 25° 10', W long. 78° 11'. It is low and swampy, but thickly wooded with pine, mangrove, cedar, and satin-wood. There is no harbour, and the pop. is collected near the NW point. It is surrounded by small islands, apparently in process of formation, and mud-shoals with barely 3 feet of water on them. Sponges are extensively fished on the W coast. Turtle, guanos, and land-crabs are abundant.

ANDROSCOGGIN, or AMERISCOGGIN, a river of the United States which, rising in N lat. 45° 12', W long. 71° 15', flows into and forms the outlet of Umbagog lake, and has the first part of its course in the eastern part of New Hampshire, After entering the state of Maine, it flows first in an easterly, and afterwards in a southerly direction; and joins the Kennebec at Merry-meeting bay, 6 m. above Bath, and 18 m. above the entrance of that river into the ocean. Its whole course is about 150 m. The tide ascends 35 m.—Darby.
ANDROWSKAIA, one of a chain of military posts erected by the empress Catherine on Mount Caucasus. It stands on the shore of a lake, 10 m. SSE of Alexandrovsk.

ANDRU'SSA, or ANDRUTZA, a town of Greece in. the Morea, 20 m. NE of Navarino, on the edge of the Messenian plain. It is a poor decayed place, and when Leake visited it, was inhabited by 250 or 300 Turkish families, and 3 or 4 Greek.
ANDRUSSOF, a town of Russia, in the gov. of
ANDRU'VISTA, a bishopric of Greece, in the outer Mani of Laconia, containing about 700 houses, of which the principal collection is in the town of the same name.
ANDRYCHAU, or Andrichow, a town of Austria, prov. of Galicia, 30 m. SW of Cracow, on the Wieprzoroka. It is the seat of a signiory, and has a castle. Pop. 3,000.
ANDRZEJEWO, a town of Poland, waivode of Plock, 22 m. E of Pultusk, on the Broczek, an affluent of the Bug. Pop. 500.
ANDUIES, a tribe of American Indians in Ecuador, between the rivers Aharicu and Putumayo.

ANDUJAR, or ANDUXAR, a partidos and city of Spain, in Andalusia, prov. of Jaen, in a fertile plain, on the r. bank of the Guadalquivir, at the base of the Sierra Morena; 19 m. NNW of Jaen; 14 m. from Aldea del Rio; in N lat. 38°, W long. 4°. Pop. about 10,000. It is in a confined situation, and reckoned an unhealthy place. Scott calls it "a dilapidated, impoverished-looking place." The chief trade is in silk. There are some tanneries and soapworks in the town. The adjacent country is fertile in corn, oil, wine, honey, and fruit. Here, of a white clay found in the vicinity, are made the celebrated light Spanish wine-coolers called alcarrazas. Near the city is a bridge of 9 arches over the Guadalquivir. A. is encompassed by old Roman walls; and is said to have borne the names of Illiturgi and Forum Julium; but Florez supposes it to be Ipasturgi.
ANDULONG, a peak in the island of Ovolau, in the Fiji group, alt. 2,070 ft. It is overtopped by about 200 ft. by the neighbouring peak of Dilleovolau.

ANDUZE, a canton, commune, and town of France, dep. of Gard. The cant., comprising 8 com., had a pop. of 9,205 in 1831. The com. and town are on the r. bank of the Gardon, 6 m. SW of Alais, 22 NW of Nismes. Pop. 5,554, mostly Protestants. The chief manufactures consist of woollens, stockings, and hats; for which a ready market is found at the great fair of Beaucaire.
ANDYK, a village of North Holland, on the shore of the Zuyder Zee, 4 m. NW of Enkhuizen.
ANED (DEL), a watering-place in the Nubian desert, on the route between Essuan and Goos; 45 m. S of Chiggre, in N lat. 20° 24'.

ANEGADA, a small isle, the most northern of the Virgin group, in the Atlantic ocean. It is 10 m. in length, and 4 m. in breadth. A few families find subsistence upon it; but it is comparatively barren, has no fresh water, and is so low as to be nearly covered at high water. The SE point is in N lat. 18° 44', W long. 64° 16'. On the eastern side is a destructive reef. - Also a bay in the straits of Magalhaen, between the bay of St. Martin and Cape St. Andrews.—Also a bay on the coast of Patagonia, at the entrance of the Rio de los Sauces.-Also an island in the gulf of Mexico, near the coast of Vera Cruz.
ANEGADIZOS, a river of Colombia, rising in Choco, and running due W into the Pacific near Point Salinas, in N lat. 6°.
ANEKELOHE, a town of Hanover, prov. of Bremen, in a marsh between two lakes communicating with the Medem and Gosche rivers.
ANEMODHURI, a village of Greece, on the summit of the ridge of Tjimbaru, on the road from Arcadia to Tripolitza.
ANEMUR. See ANAMUR.
ANERI, a town of Japan, in Nifon, 95 m. W of Miaco.

ANET, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Eure-et-Loire, arrond. of Dreux. The cant. comprises 22 com., and had a pop. of 12,369 in 1831. The com. and town are 8 m. NE of Dreux. Pop. 1,416. Considerable ironworks are wrought in 1,546. ANG the vicinity.-Also a large village of Berne, on the high road between Arberg and Neuchâtel, 17 m. NW of Berne. Pop. 2,300.
ANETZ, a commune of France, in the dep. of Loire-Inferieure, cant. of Ancenis. Pop. 1,244.
ANFA, or ANFE, a small and decayed seaport of Marocco, 48 m. SW of Sallee.
ANFELDEN, a town of Bavaria, prov. of Rezat, 12 m. NW of Anspach.

ANGAD, or Ang-gadd, an extensive tract of country, S of Algiers, nearly 90 m. from E to W, and 60 m. from N to S. It supports a large population of warlike Arabs, who extend their hostilities to the very walls of Tlemsen. The chief villages or towns are Gojeeda, Nisrag, Tenzegret, and Zezil.
ANGALA, a town of Bornu, at the southern extremity of Lake Tchad, in N lat. 12° 25'.
ANGALEWHA, a town of Bornu, prov. of Kanem, on the southern shore of Lake Tchad, in N lat. 14° 12'.

ANGANGA, an island in the Fiji group, about 30 m. from Mbua bay. It is high, broken, and uninhabited. ANGA'R, Anga'm, or Hinjam, an island in the Persian gulf, 6 m. in circuit, to the S of Kishm, and 30 m. E of Bassadore, in N lat. 26° 38', E long. 55° 46'. It is now barren and desolate, though it appears to have at one time had a considerable pop. Salt-pits and mines abound in the island; and wild goats are numerous.

ANGARA, or Angora, a river of Siberia, which issuing from the SW side of Lake Baikal passes by Irkutsk, and after a course of about 300 m. joins the Oka. The united stream, called Tungusga, or Tonguska, falls into the Yenesei, or rather is joined by that river, in about N lat. 58° 2', E long. 93° 50'. It is navigable, though impeded by cataracts. That part of the river which falls into the Baikal has a course of 700 versts, and is known as the Upper A. The Lower A. issues from the Baikal, 61 versts or 40 m. from the town of Irkutsk, in a stream 2 versts wide and 10 ft. deep; but is almost immediately of a great depth and breadth. In two places near that town its width varies from 700 to 2,000 ft., while its depth is about 60 ft. As Irkutsk is 325 ft. below the level of the Baikal, [Erman,] the descent of the stream to that place must be very rapid. Bell of Antermony travelled along the course of this river.

ANGARAES, a prov. of Peru; bounded on the N by the prov. of Jauja; on the E by Huanata; and on the W by the Andes. It is upwards of 70 m. in length from E to W; and about 12 m. in width. It is traversed by some of the streams which fall into the Apurimac, which joining the Amazon, gives the prov. the advantage of a communication with the Atlantic. It contains about 30 Indian settlements or villages. Wheat, maize, and other grain are largely cultivated; also the sugar-cane, and various fruits. Guancabelica, the cap., is situated in S lat. 12° 55'.
ANGASA, an island in the Fiji group, enclosed with three smaller islands, and several islets in one extensive reef.
ANGBAKWAH, an island in the bay of Bengal, at the mouth of the river of Arracan, in N lat. 20° 16'.
ANGE', a commune of France, in the dep. of Loir-et-Cher, cant. of Montrichard. Pop. 650.
ANGECOURT, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Ardennes, cant. of Raucourt, 5 m. SW of Sedan. Pop. 481.
ANGEDIVA, or ANJEDIVA, a small island in the Indian ocean, 2 m. off the coast of Canara, in N lat. 15° 30′, E long. 74°.
ANGEJA, a town of Brazil, prov. of Matto Grosso, on the l. bank of the Arraguaya.—Also a town of Portugal, 13 m. NE of Aveiro, on the Caima. Pop.
ANGELBERG, a small town of Bavaria, in the circle of the Upper Danube, between the Mindel and Werrack rivers. Pop. 620.
ANGEL (EL), a town of Ecuador, on the r. bank of the Mera, 52 m. NNE of Quito.
ANGEL (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Corrèze, cant. of Ussel. Pop. 1,499. Granite is quarried here.

ANGELES (Los), a frontier town of Chili, situated nearly in the centre of an immense plain extending to the Biobio river, about 9 m. distant, on one side, and to ranges of hills of moderate height on the other. It is built upon the same plan as Concepcion, with wide streets at right angles to each other. Through the centre of the town runs a clear stream from the Andes, whose snowy peaks are seen from this town soaring far above the clouds.
ANGELI, a town of Venetian Lombardy, 2 m. W of Mantua. Pop. 256.
ANGELICA, a town of New York, U.S., co. of Alleghany, of which it is the cap., on a branch of the Genessee, 40 m. W of Bath; 262 m. W of Albany. Pop. in 1836, 998; in 1841, 1,257.
ANGELINA, a small river of Mexico, in the prov. of Potosi, falling into the Trinidad about 250 m. from its mouth.—Also a town of Hayti, on the r. bank of the Yuna, 6 m. N of Cotui.
ANGELL, a river of North Wales, in Merionethshire, running into the Dyfi.

ANGELN, or ANGLEN, a district of Denmark, Morawa, 8 m. S of St. Polten. Pop. 400.—Also a on the east coast of Sleswick, between Flensborg bay and the Schlei river. It is about 18 m. in length, and the same in breadth. The soil is fertile, especially in the eastern districts. Pop. about 30,000. German historians say that the inhabitants of this district are descended from the Angles, a German nation, who dwelt in what is now the dukedom of Magdeburg in Prussia, near the Elbe, and who probably succeeded to the former seat of the Lombards when these latter had driven the Cherusci from the northern half of their country. As they never approached the Rhine and the Roman frontiers, we do not find their name mentioned by Roman authors, who comprehended them, with many others, under the general name of Chauci and Saxons, until the conquest of Britain made them better known as a separate nation. In the 5th cent. they joined their northern neighbours the Saxons, and, under the name of Anglo-Saxons, conquered England.
ANGELO, a town of Russia, gov. of Archangel, in the country of the Samoiedes, on the western shore of the estuary of the Petshora.

ANGELO-CASTRO, or ANGHELO-KASTRO, a town of Greece, in Acarnania, at the base of Mount Zigos, 11 m. SW of Vrachori. Between this town and that of Vrachori, lie two extensive lakes, or rather marshes, respectively designated from these towns. These lakes are shallow, especially that of A., and are filled with tall reeds. They abound in fish and eels.

ANGELO (SAN), a town of Brazil, prov. of Araguay, near the confluence of the Hyjuhy and Hyjuhy-Mirim. Pop. 1,960, chiefly Indians.—Also a town of Venetian Lombardy, 7 m. SW of Lodi, on the Lambro. Pop. 6,000.—There is another town of the name in the prov. of Padua. Pop. 1,736.—A town of Naples, in the prov. of Capitanata, on a high mountain of the same name, 10 m. NNE of Lauvia. Pop. 11,500. It is irregularly built; and its narrow dirty streets rise on different levels. It possesses a singularly-built subterranean church, that of St. Michael, of great fame and sanctity.
ANGELO (CIVITA DI SAN), a small town and marquisate of Naples, in Abruzzo Ultra, 50 m. ENE of Aquila.

ANGELO DE'LOMBARDI (SAN), a small town ANG of Naples, in the Principato Ultra. It was almost destroyed by an earthquake in 1664. It is 6 m. NW of Conza. Pop. 6,400. It is the seat of the bishop of San Angelo at Biscaccia.
ANGELO IN VADO (SAN), a town in the States of the Church, duchy of Urbino, on the Metauro, 12 m. SW of Urbino. It is the see of the bishop of San Angelo in Vado et Urbania. Pop. 2,500.

ANGELO (Santo), a lofty peak in the mountainrange which borders the bay of Naples. It is higher than Vesuvius. Snow lies all the year on its deep clefts; and it in fact forms the ice-house which supplies the capital throughout the year; the ice or snow being thrown into some natural caves, from which it is afterwards taken as required.

ANGELOS (LA REYNA DE LOS), a town in Upper California, 8 m. from the mission of San Gabriel, and 20 m. from San Pedro on the Pacific. Pop. 1,500. The vicinity is laid out in vineyards and maize-fields, and is level and highly fertile. Near this town there are said to be rich gold ores.
ANGENILA, a small island in the Indian seas, at the eastern entrance of the straits of Sunda, in S lat. 5° 48′, E long. 106° 38′.

ANGER, a market-town of Styria, in the circle of Gratz, on the Feistritz. Pop. 600. A considerable quantity of linen is manufactured here. It is 12 m. NNE of Gratz.—Also a market-town in Lower Austria, in the quarter of Lower Manhartsberg, on the hamlet in Upper Bavaria, 2 m. from Murnau.
ANGERAP, a river of Prussia, in the circle of Gumbrunen, one of the upper branches of the Pregel, to which it carries the waters of the Mauersee.
ANGERBACH, a river of Prussia, in Brandenburg, which falls into the Havel near Potsdam .- Also a village of Hesse-Darmstadt. Pop. 990.
ANGERBURG, a town in East Prussia, on the Angerap, near the Grossmauer lake. A considerable trade is carried on here in linen. Pop. 2,471.
ANGEREE POINT, a cape on the N coast of Java, at the mouth of the river Angeree.

ANGERMANN-AA, or ANGERMANN-ELF, a river of Sweden, which rises in the Kultsyön lake, in the prov. of Asele-Lappmark, on the Norwegian frontier, and runs in a SE, S, and SSE course into the gulf of Bothnia, 12 m. N of Hernösand, after a course of 240 m. It is a noble river, and receives the tribute of numerous lakes and streams. At Wada it is 13 m. in breadth, and contains many beautiful islands. Clarke says, "the Rhine exhibits nothing grander; nor are the banks of that river anywhere more beautifully adorned." [Travels, vol. ix.] It is navigable to Sollefta, nearly 60 m.

ANGERMANNLAND, a district of Sweden, now forming, with Medelpad, the laen or prov. of Hernösand. It lies between the parallels of 62° 10' and 64° 20' N; and 15° 40' and 19° 20' E long. Superficial area 9,508 sq. m. Its length along the coast is about 140 m. On the N it is bounded by Western Bothnia; on the E by the gulf of Bothnia; on the S by Helsingland or Gefle-laen; and on the W by Yamtland. Pop. in 1810, 42,350; in 1830, 78,821. The inhabitants of A. are remarkable for their strength and great stature. The dress of the men is universally a uniform suit of grey cloth, blue worsted stockings, a coloured red and white neckcloth, and a hat of the colour of their clothes [Clarke]. The surface of A. is diversified and beautifully picturesque. Its coasts are intersected by several deep bays and fiords, and fringed by a series of barren rocky islets. Its lakes are so numerous as to occupy one-tenth of the surface. The arable land does not exceed 56 sq. m.; the meadow-land, 281 sq. m.; the remainder consists of mountains, heaths, and forests. Rye, barley, and oats are grown; wheat seldom succeeds. The principal rivers are the Angermann-elf, the Ljingan-elf, and the Indals-elf. The chief towns are Hernösand and Sundswall.

ANGERMUNDE, a district of Prussian Brandenburg. Pop. 25,000. Its cap., of the same name, 40 m. NNE of Berlin, had a pop., in 1837, of 3,607.— There is a town of the same name in the prov. of the Rhine, 6 m. N of Dusseldorf. Pop. in 1837, 1,258. —Also a town of Russia, in Courland, on the Irbe.
ANGERN, a neat village of Prussian Saxony, in Magdeburg, 10 m. NNE of Wolmerstädt, with a castle, and 900 inhabitants.

ANGERS, a town of France, the cap. of the dep. of Maine and Loire; in N lat. 47° 28' 17", and E long. 0° 33′ 34" [Annuaire, 1848]; 178 m. SW of Paris. It rises on a gentle acclivity on the l. or E bank of the Maine or Mayenne, 5 m. above its confluence with the Loire. In the older parts of the town the streets are narrow, and the gable-faced houses, built of wood, and covered on the sides as well as roofs with slate, have a singularly gloomy aspect; but the roofs are sometimes projecting, and supported by galleries adorned with vines; and of late years a number of regularly built modern houses have been substituted for these antique structures. The more modern buildings—which occupy the site of the ancient ramparts—are generally substantial and handsome; and the adjacent public walks, especially that which terminates in the Champ-de-Mars, and the Bout-du-Monde, are very pleasing. The castle, now used as a prison and powder-magazine, occupies a commanding position on the summit of a steep rock, the base of which is washed by the river. Its walls are of great height and thickness, and were formerly flanked by massive round towers, of which all except one are now in ruins. The side next the town is defended by a moat. In the interior are the palace and chapel of King René of Anjou. The cathedral, a fine structure of the 14th century, stands on an eminence in the centre of the town. It has two lofty spires, and its interior architecture exhibits much delicate masonry. Among other public buildings are the church of the Trinity, built in 1062, and one of the finest edifices in France; the church of St. Serge, erected towards the middle of the 11th cent.; and the town-house, founded in 1155 by Henry II. of England, formerly the residence of the counts of Anjou. A. likewise contains two theatres, a library of 28,000 vols., a picture-gallery, a museum, a bank, a university, academy, a royal college, a school of arts and sciences, a deaf and dumb institution, a chamber of commerce, a botanic garden, and 5 hospitals. The Earl of Chatham and the Duke of Wellington received part of their education at the military college here, now removed to Saumur. Its riding-school was once unique in the world for its reputation and the rank of its scholars. The education of an English gentleman, in Queen Anne's time, was not considered complete till he had been instructed in the grand manège at A. -The commerce of A. consists chiefly in white wines, brandy, corn, fresh and dried fruits, honey, oil, sealing wax, wax-candles, slates—of which 80,000,000 are annually furnished by the neighbouring quarries, giving employment to 3,000 men-lint, hemp, mercury, and various articles of local manufacture, amongst which are printed calicoes, cotton and linen handkerchiefs, bolting-cloth, and sail-cloth. It also possesses some sugar-refineries, and a wax-bleaching establishment. The environs afford considerable quantities of coal and timber. Steamers ply daily between A. and Nantes, Tours, and Orleans. A. is the see of a bishop, a suffragan of Tours, whose diocese comprehends the dep. of Maine-et-Loire, ANG court.—The origin of this town, the ancient Juliomagus, is lost in antiquity. It was the capital of the Andecaves. Under the Roman supremacy, when it was embellished by numerous edifices, some of which still exist, its name was changed to Andegavia, whence Anjou. In the 5th cent. it was besieged by Childeric; and in the 9th by the Normans. It has been repeatedly taken and retaken by the Bretons, the English, and the French. In 1585 the castle was surprised by the Huguenots; and in 1793 an unsuccessful attempt upon the town was made by the Vendéans. It was the birth-place of Boden and Menage, and of the traveller Bernier.—The pop. of the com. and city in 1789 was 27,596; in 1836, 35,901; in 1846, 40,628. Before the revocation of the edict of Nantes it amounted to 50,000.—The cant. of A., comprising 21 com., had a pop. in 1831 of 51,022. The arrond. of A., which presents a superficial area of 158,997 hectares, is divided into the 7 cantons of Angers, Briollay, Ponts-de-Cé, Chalonnes, St. Georges and the Louroux, with a pop. in 1836 of 138,459.
ANGERSBACH, a town of Hesse-Darmstadt, 2 m. S of Lauterbach. Pop. 1,000.
ANGERSLEIGH, a parish of Somersetshire, 5 m. SSW of Taunton. Area 340 acres. Pop. 42.
ANGERSTEIN, a village of Hanover, 2 m. NNE of Bovenden. Pop. 550.
ANGERVILLE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Seine-et-Oise, cant. of Meréville. Pop. 1,528.
ANGERVILLE-LA-MARTEL, a commune of France, in the dep. of Seine-Inférieure, cant. of Valmont. Pop. 1,385.
ANGERVILLE - L'ORCHER, a commune of France, in the dep. of Seine-Inférieure, cant. of Criquetot-Lesneval. Pop. 1,132.
ANGESA, a town of Sweden, in N Bothnia, on the Lika, N of Luleä.
ANGEVILLERS, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Moselle, cant. of Cattenom. Pop. 755.

ANGHIARI, a village of Lombardy, in the gov. of Venice, 3 m. NW of Legnago, on the r. bank of the Adige. The French and Austrians encountered each other here on the 14th of January 1796.—Also a town of Tuscany, 14 m. NE of Arezzo. Pop. 1,449. Cutlery and fire-arms are manufactured here.
ANGHISTA, a town of Turkey, in Macedonia, on a river of the same name, 18 m. ESE of Seres, in N lat. 41° 1'.
ANGIENS, a commune of France, dep. of Seine- Inférieure, cant. of Fontaine-le-Dun. Pop. 1,012.
